The last customer activation blueprint, number six is price. You're too expensive. They don't come back because you're too expensive. What's really is important about that is it's not reason one, two, three, four, or five. See most people think the reason why people don't come back is because you are too expensive. It is really, it is in the top six, but it's this number six in the top six. So price is not the reason why most people don't come back. Next one is move a lot of people seem to eat close to where they live. So people move away. So that's reason number five. Reason number four is lousy service. They just weren't treated correctly. Reason number three is a lousy product. They were given a product that they were unhappy with. Reason number two is they found somebody new. Reason number one, however, is the fact that they just forgot. They forgot to come back. And which is really interesting about this is because if you look at the six top reasons why people don't return, six top, which is the one that is most in your control in order to get them to return? Is number one, is that's in your control? Because here's the deal is it's not your customer's job to remember to do business with you. It's your job to remind them. See because we're all busy people. So if we are customers of other folks, we're not thinking about their business. We're thinking about our own business. We're thinking about our own families. We're thinking about all the things that are going on in our life. We're not thinking about others. The number one reason why they don't come back is not because they're unhappy with the product and service that you deliver to them. It's just that they forgot. You're not on their mind. So to Dan's point, there's two pieces about this. Piece number one is you need to invite them back. And piece number two is the fact that they are embarrassed in most cases, which is going to hold them back from coming. So the invitation when you do that takes away the embarrassment. So who has lost customers in their business? As a matter of fact, just about every business that you actually work with has lost customers in them. Maybe the only exception to that is if you're in the funeral business. Because once you lose that person, it's hard to bury them again. So lost customers, let's talk about what lost customers are so that we define it. Customers who have bought from you in the past, but not return when expected. Now that's a key phrase, not return when expected. So here's something that you have to figure out. Here's something that you have to ask yourself. How often do I want my customers to buy from me again? How often do I want my customers to buy from me again? So when I was in menswear business, we looked at, we wanted to see a customer every six months. If I was in auto repair, and you guys can jump in, I'm going to guess once every three months. Does that sound about right? Auto repair, once every three months. If I'm in the restaurant business, how often do I want to see you again? And I don't see somebody within 30 days. They are now a lost customer. Dental practice, what do they tell you? Every six months, you've got to come in for a check out, right? So one of the things you've got to think about is when is somebody lost to you? It's a very important number that you've got to know. Most people don't think about it enough. As a matter of fact, most people don't think about lost customers enough, period. And the reason why you really want to think about them enough is because they are the third easiest customer to get to come back to your business. Now by the way, when I use the word customer, it's interchangeable with patients and clients. So it's lost customers, clients, patients, but for the sake of this, we're going to just use the word customers. But you want to think about it. It is the third easiest person to get to come back to your business. Number one is what? Number one is what? The current customer that you got, the current customer. What's number two? Referral, somebody who refers to you is the second easiest customer to get. But the third easiest one to get is the one that was a previous customer who hasn't come back when they are expected to come back. The sad thing about this is where do most people spend most of their money and time with customers getting them to their business? New customers cost at least five times more to attract than previous customers. So you got to figure out, as I said, when a customer is lost. You got to be thinking that to yourself, when do I stop seeing a student in my martial arts school? When do I stop? At what point are they now lost? So most people, what they do is, is they put their lost customers in their general marketing campaigns. They just dump them all into their general stuff. And they treat them like they treat active customers. But the better result you'll get is when you let them actually know that we miss seeing you. I'm going to show you tons of examples. And also the best method to get customers to respond is, number one is determine when they're lost. Number two is let them know that they are lost. And number three is use something called sequential marketing. When I first started doing this in my menswear stores, I only used one media which was what? Direct mail. And then I added voice broadcast. And now if I was still in that business, I would probably substitute voice broadcasts for email marketing. But I would use different media because different people respond to different media. Quite frankly, the more different media that you do use, usually the better the result because different people do respond to different media. Just letting them know that their loss is not good enough. You've got to always use in your marketing direct response strategies. So you have to have an offer, a deadline, a guarantee, a headline to grab their attention, testimonials to prove. Better the best testimonials to use in lost customer reactivation campaigns. Testimonials from people who were previously lost. You show testimonials, you know, I foolishly stopped getting my car repaired, an ABC auto repair. And it was the biggest mistake I ever made. Because I ignored my car, it actually wind up costing me $3,800 in unnecessary repairs that had I taken my car back to them every three months, I could have avoided that. Now that message works in any business at all. So testimonial that actually speaks to the fact of the person who was previously lost and the problems that were created by that. Personalization, grabbers, premiums, photos, of course, the copy doodles. So you want to use direct response techniques in all of this marketing. So the lost customer reactivation examples. Omaha State, this was a self-mailer that I got this in the mail. And when you open it up in there, there was an offer for $20 of a free gift. There was an offer with a deadline in there. But what's important about this is we want you back and I'll make it worth you while. What did they identify to me? They identified to me that I am lost. I am a lost customer. It wasn't the kind of mail that just their regular customers would get. It's somebody who hasn't bought from them in the time that they expected me to buy from them again. Here's another example. Golf magazine, reinstatement mailing, sent this out. This is not, I don't know what happened to golf, but this is just an example of their thing, which actually is a reinstatement form to reinstate this person. Thank you. Thank you very much. I reinstate this person of golf subscription. I show you these big, big companies for a reason. They're doing it. It must be worthwhile to do it and I can assure you it's going to be worthwhile to do it as well. Here's another one. PriceLine.com, your $50 bonus cash certificate email. This is an email that goes out to people that have purchased on PriceLine in the past, but when triggered, when they haven't had a purchase again, they send this out to them with, you see there, it says, we miss you. Here's another one. Free conference calling email. This is from free conference calling. We want you back. You see a trend to this? They're identifying the fact that the person is missing. Person gets this. They'll be thinking to themselves, my God, number one, is they care enough about me to come after me, and number two is, I should be buying from them again because it's not their job to remember to buy. Again, it's your job to remind them. Here's the campaign that was used actually for many, many years, probably close to six years in my Gage Men's Wore Store, that what we would do is we would mail out to them after somebody hit month number seven. If they didn't come back by the first six months, from the last time they were there, we would automatically put them into a lost customer reactivation campaign. Consistently, we got 13% of all people that were on the lost customer list that month would come back after the campaign. It was a game changer for us. The other thing that's important about this is the fact that every month there was new people that went into this campaign. If you're looking for a steady stream of new money, new income coming to your business, it is very smart to create a lost customer campaign that just keeps rolling month after month after month. I will tell you, I'm going to show you the whole campaign. It was a four-step campaign. Don't think it's just we sent out something and all of a sudden 13% of them came back because you usually have to work harder at bringing them back with sequential marketing. So a lot of people are going to be asking themselves, what happens to, in this particular case, the 87% that didn't come back? What I would do with the 87% that didn't come back in my menswear stores, which I'd advise all you to do, is wait another period of time, which I did, was wait another six months. So on month 13th, I put them in the same campaign again. And that would bring another 13% of them back to life again. So sometimes when people don't come back, it's just that they're just not ready. So I would put them in a second campaign. We're going to deal with that towards the end of this talk to give you really my best advice on how to really deal with people who just stubbornly will not come back. But here's the campaign. So the first thing was a six-by-nine-inch postcard that went out and says, we really do miss you. I read the tellment, it's been six months. It's been too long. And we gave them a free $10 offer just for coming into the store. That's it, with a deadline. And our deadline was always two weeks, 14 days was my deadline. Tested a lot of different deadlines. And this particular campaign, by the way, on this particular campaign, 14 days worked the best. And we tested 10 days, we tested seven days, we actually tested 21 days, 14 days was the best. So you got to test different deadlines to see which one works the best. This was one side of the card, here was the other side of the card. By the way, you'll notice that it's personalized. Steve, we miss you. Personalization will increase your response. If they didn't come back within two weeks, we sent them out of second postcard. So we send out the search party. And what's in there is basically the same thing as in the first postcard, although we changed the graphics. But there's one key component part that's in there that you must, must, must do, which is you have to refer to the fact that approximately two weeks ago, we sent you a postcard with a carrot on it. As you move people down sequential mailing, you always want to refer to the second, the previous mailing that you sent out. If they didn't respond two weeks later, we sent them out the third postcard, which is last chance. And in there, what we do is previously, we sent you two postcards, one of them with a carrot on it, the other one with a compass on it. And we haven't seen you. So we're going to give you an absolute last chance to come back to us with this special offer. This is one side, and here's the other side. And then we said to them in this postcard, if we don't hear from you, you will never hear from us again. Then three days later, we sent them out an email. Back then, I used to send them out a voice broadcast. In this particular case, I would now use an email. The parts that are highlighted are the parts that just change, but the email is a basic email that went out to them. And this is the type of campaign, although back then I was using voice broadcasts, would get me 13% response on a regular basis. Moving them from direct mail to the phone. Phone call from the store manager offered them a free week about a limited tanning. They stop in with the next four days. The phone calls are very well received, and the clients greatly appreciate it. If we get an answer machine, we leave a message saying that we have a free gift for them. If they call us back, this is a new step we have just added and has been working well. Cost is zero since they're using their existing staff to make the calls. And the fifth step is bringing in an additional 45% of the list by adding a different kind of media, which in this particular case is outbound phone call. And many of you have staff that could actually be doing these things, adding phone. Phone to email. This is from Platinum Mastermind member Sandy Krakowski. So this is for her coaching program to reactivate people. And I'll read you a little bit of it here. It's a hey first name, how's it going? You know life happens, doesn't it? Things pile up, and before we know it, there's not a few things on the to do list that aren't getting done. There's a month worth of projects that we're gotten off track with. I totally know what this is like and why I spend regular time with my coaches making sure derailing doesn't happen. The fact of the matter is the only difference between a millionaire and a wannabe business owner is they know how to prevent derailing and getting off track. We're here to support you and believe in your dreams. Here's a copy of last night's coaching group call, no charge, my gift to you, link to the call. Your dreams are important to me. Let's get back on track. Link to reactivate coaching with love. And you guys think that's a good email to go out? That's a spectacular email to go out. And it's very, very conversational, very, very conversational. And you see that all the blame was taken off of the fact that they quit and put on other factors. So again, it's very similar to as I told you the outbound phone call that we made at my men's restore saying that, hey listen, I know you didn't make your time that we had set aside today. I'm just assuming something came up. It's happened to me also. So let's just get back together again. It takes the blame off of them because most people are embarrassed when they stop doing the things that you want them to do. So it's a very, very smart strategy to send out to them. Plus the fact that you gave them a free call, which is also a reciprocity piece. Now step number one is the brown bag that goes out. I told you I put it in the hall of fame and the outrageous talk. The brown bag that goes out. Inside there's a letter that goes in the brown bag. Step number two is the postcard we've been searching for you. And step number three, I actually took the postcard and created it into a step number three email. So a couple helpful hints. Number one, ask the lost customer what can we do to win you back? So after you put them in a lost customer campaign and they're like, what I do with my menswear business, I did it once and then six months later I did it again. Maybe the reason why they didn't come back is one of the top six reasons. Well not the top six, it's reasons number two through six. Maybe they didn't come back because they had a bad experience with you. Maybe they didn't come back because they moved away. Maybe they didn't come back because your prices were too high. But none of those reasons why they didn't come back, you have an opportunity to overcome the objection because unless you ask, you don't know what the objection is. So if they don't come back for the regular loss campaign, ask them why they didn't come back. That's what you do to win you back. Then you got to listen and be open to what lost customers tell you. You got to listen to that and you got to be open to what they tell you because sometimes you got to go fix the other problem that's in your business. So if now you can meet their requirement or you can overcome their objection, then ask them to come back again and then at that point you probably need to do something on a special offer to them to bring them back but they will really, really, really thank you for doing that for them. If you win those people back, it's going to be really hard for you to lose those people because you really went the extra mile which nobody else does. Continue to keep in touch with lost customers. So what is the best media to keep in touch with people on a monthly basis? Hint. Newsletters. Everybody should be in a newsletter with you to keep in touch with them. Next is have multiple front end offers for lost customers to return to you. Most important point about reviving lost customers. What is the overall most important point about reviving lost customers? It's this. Have a strategic lost customer activation plan in place. So there's nothing else that I can impress upon you while you are here is if you want to get back the third easiest customers to get to your business, what you want to do is you want to, as you're sitting here and seeing these examples I show you, you want to go through the process. Number one is identifying in your business when are your customers lost. Number two is you got to let the customers know that they are lost. And number three is you want to put them into a lost customer activation campaign based on when they are lost and you do it multi-step sequentially and you want to make, you want to systematize it. You want to make a system. So like I said to you in my menswear stores every month whoever hit that number, number seven, they got into that campaign. Okay. So that is that story on the lost customer activation campaign.
